steps.title: Recognize the inverse relationship
steps.content: The study found a broad inverse relationship: as market share increases, the increase in cross-category purchase probability decreases, and vice versa. What accounts for this pattern?

steps.title: Identify the ceiling-and-floor mechanism
steps.content:
Choice C explains this inverse relationship through different ceiling and floor effects. High-market-share brands already have high purchase probability with or without the cross-category effect, so there's minimal room for additional increase. Low-market-share brands have low baseline probability, but when purchased in one category, signal consumer preference, creating substantial room to increase in another.

The other choices either address single-category competitive dynamics (A, B) or misattribute low market share to non-recognition (D), neither of which explains why the cross-category effect is systematically stronger for lower-market-share brands.

That matches choice C — C is correct.
